segments.travel_time: 2 days by packet boat (1830s-1840s), faster by line boat after improvements
segments.cost: $1-$2 deck passage, $3-$4 cabin passage for this segment (1830s)
segments.conditions: Horse-drawn packet boats glide at four miles per hour through the Mohawk Valley, tow ropes stretching to teams plodding along the towpath. Deck passengers crowd the open cargo hold with their trunks and bundles, sleeping wherever they can find space among barrels of flour and bales of cloth. Low stone bridges appear every mile or so and the steersman shouts the warning that became a folk song — 'Low bridge, everybody down!' — and anyone standing on deck who fails to duck risks a broken skull.

segments.cost: included in through-passage; total Albany to Buffalo approximately $3-$5 deck, $7-$10 cabin (1830s)
segments.conditions: The canal passes through the salt marshes around Syracuse, where the air carries the mineral tang of the Onondaga salt works. Mosquitoes swarm the boat at dusk. Cabin passengers retreat below to bunks stacked three high in a space barely five feet wide, while deck passengers swat insects and watch the towpath lanterns of passing boats reflect off black water.

West of Syracuse the canal climbs through a staircase of locks — the boat rises, water rushes in, the heavy wooden gates creak open, and the horses lean into the towrope again. Passengers watch the landscape shift from swamp to rolling farmland. At every lock, local vendors crowd the banks selling bread, cheese, cider, and whiskey to passengers who have been eating salt pork for days.

The final stretch runs through the flatlands west of Rochester where the canal is arrow-straight for miles. Buffalo appears as a haze of woodsmoke and the smell of Lake Erie — fish, tar, and fresh-sawn lumber. At the Buffalo terminus, immigrants transfer to Great Lakes steamers bound for Cleveland, Detroit, Chicago, and Milwaukee, the canal packet giving way to the open lake and a new kind of journey entirely.

who_used_it: Irish famine immigrants (1845-1855) heading to upstate New York canal towns and onward to the Midwest via the Great Lakes. German immigrants bound for Buffalo, Cleveland, Detroit, Chicago, and Milwaukee. New England families relocating to the 'burned-over district' and the Western Reserve of Ohio. After 1850, Scandinavian immigrants increasingly used the canal-to-lakes route to reach Wisconsin and Minnesota.
narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name} likely traveled the Erie Canal from {origin} toward {destination}, riding a packet boat at walking speed through 363 miles of locks, low bridges, and towpath country — the slow, cheap, remarkably safe water road that funneled a million immigrants into the American interior.
source_references: Peter L. Bernstein, 'Wedding of the Waters: The Erie Canal and the Making of a Great Nation' (W.W. Norton, 2005)
Carol Sheriff, 'The Artificial River: The Erie Canal and the Paradox of Progress, 1817-1862' (Hill and Wang, 1996)
Ronald E. Shaw, 'Erie Water West: A History of the Erie Canal, 1792-1854' (University Press of Kentucky, 1966)
Gerard Koeppel, 'Bond of Union: Building the Erie Canal and the American Empire' (Da Capo Press, 2009)
